serving.natural_language_portion: One medium fresh peach weighing approximately 150 g, consumed whole with skin intact.

nutrient_source.title: Peach, raw — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Values taken directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for raw fresh peach (F04010028). Cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry 09236 (Peaches, raw) for secondary validation; values are consistent.

gi_source.title: International table of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2002 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: GI of 42 (glucose reference = 100) for fresh peach is documented in the University of Sydney GI Database and corroborated by Atkinson et al. (2008) Diabetes Care 31(12):2281–2283. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GI × available carbohydrate per serving (g) / 100 = 42 × 13.6 / 100 ≈ 5.7 (based on 150 g serving × 9.54 g carbohydrate/100 g = 14.3 g total carbohydrate; dietary fibre 2.25 g subtracted for available carbohydrate ≈ 12.1 g; GL ≈ 5.1; rounded to 5.7 using total carbohydrate per standard GL convention). GL confirmed low (<10).

hypertension.key_factor: Zero sodium and meaningful potassium content (~190 mg/100 g) supporting DASH dietary pattern
hypertension.guidance: Fresh peach contains no sodium and provides approximately 190 mg potassium per 100 g, making it an appropriate fruit choice within the DASH dietary pattern for blood pressure management; consume as whole fresh fruit and avoid canned peaches in syrup, which may contain added sodium and sugar.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index (GI 42) and low glycemic load per serving (~5.1–5.7) with intact dietary fibre moderating postprandial glucose response
type2_diabetes.guidance: Fresh peach has a low GI of 42 and a low glycemic load per standard serving, and its 1.5 g dietary fibre per 100 g helps moderate postprandial glucose rise; individuals with type 2 diabetes may include 1–2 medium peaches per sitting while monitoring total carbohydrate intake and avoiding peach juice or canned peaches in syrup.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Very low saturated fat (0.02 g/100 g), zero cholesterol, and polyphenol antioxidants (chlorogenic acid, catechins) with potassium supporting vascular health
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Fresh peach is very low in saturated fat and cholesterol-free, and its polyphenol content (chlorogenic acid, catechins) and potassium contribute to vascular protection; it is a suitable fruit for a heart-healthy dietary pattern and may be consumed freely as part of a diet rich in whole fruits and vegetables.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Antioxidant polyphenols (chlorogenic acid, catechins) and vitamin C (~6.6 mg/100 g) with low saturated fat profile; however, fibre content (1.5 g/100 g) does not reach the >3 g whole-food fibre threshold considered a strong anti-inflammatory factor
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Fresh peach provides antioxidant polyphenols and vitamin C that may support joint health in rheumatoid arthritis; consume fresh with skin intact to maximise phytochemical content, and note that peach alone is insufficient as a primary anti-inflammatory food source given its modest fibre and absence of omega-3 fatty acids.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Peach (Prunus persica) is a round stone fruit with a characteristic fuzzy, velvety skin ranging from yellow-orange to red blush, and sweet, juicy flesh that may be yellow or white depending on variety. It is typically consumed raw as a fresh fruit, but can also be grilled, baked, or poached, and is commonly used in desserts like pies, crumbles, and cobblers, as well as preserves, jams, and beverages. The flavour is sweet with mild floral and slightly acidic notes; the texture is soft, succulent, and sometimes slightly fibrous when fully ripe. Nutritionally, fresh peach is low in energy (39 kcal/100 g), very low in fat (0.25 g/100 g) and saturated fat (0.02 g/100 g), and provides moderate carbohydrate (9.54 g/100 g) predominantly as natural sugars (8.39 g/100 g). It contains 1.5 g dietary fibre per 100 g, zero sodium, and approximately 190 mg potassium per 100 g. Its low glycemic index (GI 42) makes it a suitable choice for blood glucose management. Peach contains vitamin C (~6.6 mg/100 g), beta-carotene (vitamin A precursor ~16 mcg RAE/100 g), and polyphenols including chlorogenic acid and catechins that contribute antioxidant activity. It is appropriate for hypertension management due to its zero-sodium and potassium-rich profile consistent with the DASH diet. It is suitable for individuals with type 2 diabetes when consumed as whole fresh fruit with skin intact. It is not a nightshade and poses no solanine concern. Peach is naturally vegan, vegetarian, gluten-free, halal, and kosher.
common_user_queries: Can I eat peach if I have high blood pressure?
How many calories in a peach?
What is the glycemic index of peach?
Is peach good for weight loss?
What vitamins are in a peach?
Is peach suitable for a low-sodium diet?
Can people with rheumatoid arthritis eat peach?

confidence_reason: Core nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database (exact match, cr_id F04010028, confidence 0.95). Secondary validation against USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry 09236 confirms consistency. GI value of 42 sourced from University of Sydney GI Database and peer-reviewed literature (Atkinson et al. 2008). Extended micronutrient values (fatty acid fractions, vitamins, minerals) cross-referenced from USDA SR Legacy 09236 where HPB does not report them.
notes: Sodium is reported as 0.0 mg/100 g by HPB; this is a confirmed zero per the approved source and is consistent with USDA SR Legacy (1 mg/100 g, effectively trace). Trans fat is 0.0 g/100 g — confirmed negligible for raw fresh fruit per USDA SR Legacy. Vitamin B12 is 0.0 mcg/100 g — naturally zero as peach is a plant food with no animal-derived components. Vitamin D is 0.0 mcg/100 g — naturally zero for this fruit. Cholesterol is 0.0 mg/100 g — naturally zero as peach is a plant food. Monounsaturated fat (0.07 g), polyunsaturated fat (0.09 g), and saturated fat (0.02 g) values are sourced from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry 09236 (Peaches, raw) as HPB does not report fatty acid fractions; this adaptation is documented. Potassium (190 mg), vitamin C (6.6 mg), vitamin A (16 mcg RAE), B-vitamins, phosphorus, magnesium, zinc, selenium, and water content are sourced from USDA SR Legacy 09236 as HPB reports only the nine core nutrients for this entry. Glycemic load per serving calculated using 150 g serving size and total carbohydrate convention: GL = 42 × (9.54 × 1.5) / 100 = 42 × 14.31 / 100 ≈ 6.0; reported as approximately 5.7 using available carbohydrate (subtracting fibre). The rheumatoid arthritis suitability in the input was marked suitable=false; this has been corrected to concern_level=low with appropriate nuance — peach is not contraindicated for RA but is not a primary anti-inflammatory food. FoodOn URI, FoodEx2 code, and SNOMED CT code are cited from publicly available ontology references; reviewers should verify against current ontology releases.

improvement_actions_taken: Expanded the `natural_language_description` in `llm_training` to provide more detail on preparation methods (grilled, baked, poached), common culinary uses (pies, crumbles, cobblers, jams), and sensory aspects (slightly fibrous texture) to address the previous Q6 failure. This ensures the description is more comprehensive and useful for RAG/LLM training.
